sentences.text: Good afternoon.
Lawrence L.
Rhee.
R-H-E-E.
L-A-W-R-E-N-C-E.
Middle initial, L.
No, it's just an initial.
No.
My name was Lothar.
Yes.
L-O-T-H-A-R.
It was the same.
Yeah.
No.
Everybody calls me Larry.
That's the only thing.
In July, I'll be 78.
In Recklinghausen, Germany.
R-E-C-K-L-I-N-G-H-A-U-S-E-N.

It's in the province of Westphalia, near the Dutch-Belgium border in the Rhine-Ruhr Valley, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in the Rhine-Ruhr Valley, roughly speaking, 125 miles or so north of the city of Cologne.
Yes.
My family on my father's side has been traced back to before 1800.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on my mother's side, even further, like 1750.
Recklinghausen.
At that time, Recklinghausen had about 80,000 inhabitants.
Not very well.
No, I don't recall.
But there were quite a number.
I mean, it was not-- we didn't have a Jewish community where lots of Jews lived together.
We had our synagogue, and I remember there were some Orthodox Jews.
But to the best of my knowledge, there were no Reform Jews.
It was an industrial town, you could call it.
We had coal mines there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was about the only industry that I can think of that I remember.
Mostly merchants, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] doctors, lawyers.
I remember a few.
And yeah, a pretty good mix.
Yes, I do.
Yes.
[GERMAN] 21.
No, it was an apartment.
It was above a store.
The store was on the bottom, and our apartments were on top.
Very nice, cozy-- pleasant living.
I guess we-- see, we actually lived on two floors, the floor immediately above the store, and then another floor above that, where we lived.
Yes.
Moritz.
Rhee.
He was a merchant.
One of those little general stores.
No groceries, but toys and crystalware and little bit of hardware, porcelain.
In what way do you mean?
In--
He-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was, as I say, a merchant.
He took care of the business.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a good father.
Strict, yes.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very-- very easy to get along with, really.
We used to go fishing some on the weekends, nearby rivers.
And that was our weekend.
Else.
E-L-S-E.
Seligmann.
S-E-L-I-G-M-A-N-N.
I know that during the war, she became a nurse's aide to take care of the German soldiers.
But she actually was a housewife.
That was her main occupation.
World War I.
A very kind and good person.
Nothing special.
She took care of the family and--
Still living is one brother.
He is a veteran of Bataan, the March of Death, and is retired in Mexico.
He went to live in Mexico.
Heinz L.
Older, he's older.
And I have two sisters deceased.
Anita, and the other one was Ursula.
Yes.
Both of them.
Anita was Colby, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Ursula was Johnson.
We played together, fought together, [LAUGHS] like all children do.
And but generally we got along very well.
Yes, they were younger.
Only one, my grandmother on my mother's side.
Was Bertha.
B-E-R-T-H-A.
Seligmann.
She was a wonderful person.
When she grew too old to handle her own affairs, yes, she lived with us.
No, I didn't know them at all.
Yes.
Well, my name is derived from the little town of Rheda, R-H-E-D-A, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Westphalia, also, where my forebears lived.
I don't know how many generations lived there.
But up to that particular time, around 1806 or so, the Jews were only permitted to have their Jewish names.
But my forebears got around that by calling them Rhe, so the people would know from where they came-- R-H-E.
And in those days, we spelled our name with an accent aigu over the first E and the L and the E.
That's where the double E comes in.
I-- for four years, I went to a Jewish parochial school in Recklinghausen where we had eight grades all in one room, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I studied Hebrew, Bible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then my father-- well, my parents both were not-- let's call it overly religious.
But we were Jewish and--
When I went to the parochial school, I learned to celebrate it and also started to observe Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashana and other-- Purim.
You know, we never had Passover in our home.
We were invited many times, but we never had one in our home that I could recall.
Well, they were the regular Passovers, and I enjoyed them.
We enjoyed them.
To friends.
Yes.
In the synagogue in Recklinghausen.
I had to study for it very hard and I had to memorize my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] call it a speech.
My-- and it was not a very big affair, fairly small, but--
No.
Only-- only one that I know.
And I know the Orthodox people met in somebody's home.
No, no, no.
Well, of course, all I can talk about is my own personal experience, and we got along fine.
For four years, a grammar school.
And then I went to high school for six years.
But I couldn't finish because of Hitler at that time already.
I don't know how to d-- [CHUCKLES] I think I was fairly easygoing.
I had my circle of friends, I had my due fights [LAUGHS] with the neighbor kids, you know.
But generally speaking--
We played together quite a bit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We sometimes went to other-- to the other people's home, or they came to us.
Yes.
I joined a gymnastic club when I was about eight or nine years old.
And then after Hitler came to power, I was kicked out of there.
And I was about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was about 16 or 17 years old, and then I was kicked out.
In some parts of it I was, yes.
No.
I wasn't there then.
You see, I left Germany in December 1935.
Yeah.
I wanted to become a doctor, a medical doctor.
Yeah.
You know, classmates.
And my parents had quite a group of non-Jewish friends, yes.
Not that I recall, no.
For one thing, I-- my best friend in school, my classmate, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] could no longer speak to me.
His father was a Social Democrat.
And he wound up in a concentration camp in the early-- during the early part.
He was released about six months after.
But he had been thoroughly brainwashed, and his son could no longer speak to me.
His parents would not permit me to have any contact at all, for fear that it might harm his dad.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for one, I remember one thing.
It's not a change so much, but a happening.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was in high school, two of my classmates, with whom I got along very well, by the way, had held minor leadership positions in the Hitler Youth.
And suddenly, they were taken out of school at about-- for about two or three weeks.
I forget the exact time.
And then they-- when they came back, they told me confidentially that they had been put into military camp for learning to shoot pistols, rifles, and machine guns at the age of about 16.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] The teachers, by the way, that I had did not show any anti-Semitism.
No, as I said, the kids treated me fairly well.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got along fine.
And the teachers, as I mentioned already-- they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't show any particular anti-Semitism.
No, never did.
Oh, I heard many of his speeches.
Well, here's the thing.
You see, Germany had just lived through terrible inflation.
[COUGHS] Excuse me.
And all of us thought that once Hitler could stabilize the economical situation that they would-- that he would relent on his anti-Semitism, but that didn't happen, of course.
And talking about inflation, I remember one particular incident.
In fact, I still have the coin.
Around 1923, my brother asked my mother to give him some money to buy a lollipop.
And my mother gave him 5 million marks.
And he came back crying from the grocery store across the street.
He couldn't buy a lollipop for 5 million marks.
Well, if it were nowadays, it would be $1,400,000.
He couldn't buy a lollipop for it.
The mark-- German money was worthless.
A shirt, I read someplace, cost-- I don't-- millions and millions of marks, a plain old shirt.

And I'm told-- now, I didn't see this, or at least I don't recall it-- that people went and got their wages, got their payment, and went to the banks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] having wheat barrels to hold their money.
There was so much paper, there was no other way to carry it.
But I don't recall that.
I have been told this.
Not really.
Not really.
We-- you know, we had grown up together and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had been playing together.
Not very much, no.
Not thrown out.
I couldn't continue.
Let's put-- after a certain year, after what we would call 10th grade here, which is a certain cut-off point in Germany, was a certain kind of cut-off point in Germany then.
I could go no longer.
It wouldn't have been feasible anyhow.
No.
We just didn't--
Re-enroll.
Yeah.
Yeah.
My parents were informed that was it.
I went to work on a farm.
It was a setup where you were, like, going to school, a farm school.
But these were Jewish owners.
Not in Recklinghausen.
I worked in two places.
One was in Silesia, and the other one near Berlin.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were taught a little theory in farming and also had to do the hard practical work that goes with it, like plowing, hoeing weeds.
And you mention it, and we had to do it-- threshing.
Milking cows.
No.
Oh, when I was a kid, I had a little vacation job here, there, trying to earn a little pocket money, but no job, as such, to live on.
We were very fortunate.
My mother had three brothers who had left Germany before the turn of the century.
In fact, the first one came over in 1890 or so.
To Bernalillo, New Mexico.
Bernalillo's about 20 miles north from-- of Albuquerque.
And they, of course, knew much more what was going on in Germany, due to the news media than we in Germany knew.
And they supplied us with the affidavits to be able to get the whole family into the United States.
My mother was-- my mother's trip was delayed by about a year and a half or two because she was in Switzerland.
She had undergone a lung operation.
But she was in Switzerland at the time, and-- but she joined us later.
Yes.
I guess.

It was so long ago, I-- my-- I had some relatives in Berlin, also a brother of my mother, who did all the paperwork because the consulate was there, and everything was-- all I know is that we only went to the consulate once and were interrogated a little bit, and then we got our papers.
I remember that I didn't know where Bernalillo was.
[LAUGHS] No idea.
This was in 1935.
I don't know how long the process took.
But there were quotas already.
But it took a little time, though.
But in-- we left in December 1935, spent Christmas and New Year on the high seas.
We went through the Panama Canal and landed in Los Angeles.
That's the part of the trip I remember.
Yes.
The Elbe, the German river, Elbe, was the name of the ship.
Well, you know, I really, at that time, was already on the farm.
So I had no particular-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't homesick or anything.
My dad, though, I remember, cried when he was aboard the ship.
But us kids didn't have that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that particular [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] feeling about it.
